refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency that ensures safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is mostly atomic, focusing on teleoperation latency compatible with safety. It is solution-neutral as it does not specify how latency is achieved, only that it must be compatible with safe operation. The FR is kept in the problem domain by stating the requirement in terms of latency compatibility with safety, not specifying technology or implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the end-to-end communication latency during teleoperation under agreed network conditions and verify that the 95th percentile latency (p95) is less than or equal to 150 ms, ensuring compliance with safety requirements.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency less than or equal to 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: Implement a communication protocol optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer
Design a system architecture that prioritizes real-time data processing and minimal buffering
Use a dedicated communication channel with guaranteed quality of service for teleoperation data
Incorporate predictive control algorithms to compensate for communication delays
Segment teleoperation data streams to prioritize critical control commands over less time-sensitive data
selected_dp.selected_dp: Implement a communication protocol optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer

potential_sources: Integration with other system communication protocols
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP focuses on the communication protocol design, which is logically independent from other system functions. However, it requires careful interface definition with network infrastructure and other system components to avoid coupling.

assumptions_risks: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters to meet latency requirements
Operational policies do not impose additional constraints that increase latency
Predictive control or buffering strategies are not required to meet latency targets
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Consider explicitly including a DP for system architecture design that supports real-time processing and minimal buffering to complement the communication protocol DP, ensuring robustness against variable network conditions.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Module responsible for remote control and communication between operator and vehicle

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bi-directional data flow for vehicle status and control feedback

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands and instructions from operator to vehicle

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network connection used for communication protocol

module_containment_scores.notes: Very broad module, contains many unrelated functions, leading to high diffusion of communication protocol logic.
Contains vehicle-side communication but protocol optimized for end-to-end latency involves operator side as well, causing moderate diffusion.
Most specific module handling remote control and communication, well suited to contain the communication protocol with minimal diffusion.
Focused on safety alerts, not communication protocol, poor containment and high diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most appropriate boundary to contain the low-latency communication protocol DP because it directly manages the remote control communication link between operator and vehicle, minimizing diffusion and maximizing cohesion.
containment_risks: Potential dependency on underlying network link interface may require coordination with other modules.
If communication protocol needs to interact closely with vehicle internals, some diffusion may occur.
Changes in communication protocol could impact Telemetry and Tasking interfaces, requiring careful interface management.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: If containment risks materialize, consider refining the DP to explicitly separate protocol logic from vehicle control logic, enabling clearer modular boundaries and reducing cross-module diffusion.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying a communication protocol optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer, which aligns with the requirement to ensure safe remote operation with latency p95 ≤ 150 ms.

diffusion_risks: Potential dependency on underlying network link interface may require coordination with other modules.
If communication protocol needs to interact closely with vehicle internals, some diffusion may occur.
Changes in communication protocol could impact Telemetry and Tasking interfaces, requiring careful interface management.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module has the highest containment score (9) and lowest diffusion score (2), indicating it is well suited to contain the communication protocol DP with minimal diffusion. Other modules show higher diffusion risks and lower containment, supporting the choice of Teleoperation as the IM boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the FR through the IM is feasible by measuring end-to-end communication latency within the Teleoperation module under agreed network conditions to ensure p95 ≤ 150 ms.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the communication protocol DP to the Teleoperation IM is clear and appropriate, as this module is responsible for remote control and communication between operator and vehicle, directly supporting the FR.

recommended_revisions: If containment risks materialize, consider refining the DP to explicitly separate protocol logic from vehicle control logic, enabling clearer modular boundaries and reducing cross-module diffusion.

decision.justification: All gates (FR->DP, DP->IM, FR->IM) have passed successfully, indicating that the current DP and IM adequately and feasibly satisfy the FR with clear allocation and manageable diffusion risks. The communication protocol DP directly addresses the FR, and the Teleoperation module is an appropriate IM boundary with high containment and low diffusion. Verification is feasible and allocation is clear. Given the high safety criticality, further decomposition is not warranted at this stage to avoid unnecessary complexity and maintain traceability and control.

revise.why: If containment risks materialize in the future, consider refining the DP to separate protocol logic from vehicle control logic to reduce diffusion and improve modular boundaries.

assumptions: The communication protocol DP fully satisfies the FR for safe remote operation with latency p95 ≤ 150 ms.
The Teleoperation module is capable of containing the DP with minimal diffusion.
Verification of latency within the Teleoperation module is feasible under agreed network conditions.
open_risks: Potential dependency on underlying network link interface requiring coordination with other modules.
Possible diffusion if communication protocol interacts closely with vehicle internals.
Changes in communication protocol impacting Telemetry and Tasking interfaces requiring careful interface management.
evidence_needed: Latency measurements of end-to-end communication within the Teleoperation module under operational conditions.
Monitoring of diffusion risks during integration and testing phases.
